content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Pamela A. Gaspar 
2048 Willow Drive 
El Centro, CA 
92243 
Dear Ms. Gaspar: 
October 3, 1985 
Re: Your Request for Advice, 
Our File No. A-85-195 
Thank you for your letter requestinq advice reqardinq the 
incompatibility of public offices arisinq from your possible 
position with the El Centro city Council and your position as 
an employee of a city-owned hospital. 
At this time, the city 
council sits as the hospital board. 
Our aqency is not authorized to render advice on this 
subject, as the Political Reform 'Act deals only with conflicts 
between official duties and private financial interests. 
The 
Attorney General's office is the only state aqency which qive 
advice on possible conflicts arisinq from two public offices. 
I enclose a copy of our November 1983 Bulletin, which contains 
. a listinq of several Attorney General's opinions on the subject. 
The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from 
makinq, participatinq in, or usinq his' or her official position 
to influence a qovernmental decision in which the official has 
a financial interest. 
Government Code section 87100. 
Pursuant 
to section 87103, a public official has a financial interest in 
a qovernmental decision when it is reasonably toreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinquishable from its effect on the public qenerally, on, 
amonq other interests, any source of income aqqreqatinq $250 or 
more received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
However, the term income does not include any salary and 
reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, 
local, or federal qovernment aqency. 
Government Code Section 
82030(b) (2). 
Therefore, the income you receive as an employee 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Ms. Pamela A. Gaspar 
October 3, 1985 
. 
Page 2 
of the city-owned hospital is not considered income for 
purposes of the Political Reform Act. 
Accordingly, should you be successful in your bid for a 
council seat, you would not be required to disqualify yourself 
from decisions which may affect the hospital. 
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, 
please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
JET:nwm 
Sincerely, 
cjW)U~J~~u_~ 
Jeanette E. Turvill 
Legal Assistant 
Legal Division 


SEPl'EMBER 9, 1985 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES ca1MISSION 
P.O. POX 807 
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 
95804-0804 
EXECUI'IVE/LEGAL . COUNCIL: 
I WOUID LIKE TO KlilCW IF THERE IS ANY FORM OF CONFLICT TO BE AN EMPLOYEE OF 
A CITY avNED HOSPITAL AND AT THE SAME TIME A MEMBER OF CITY COUNCIL. 
THE 
CITY COUNCIL SITS AT THIS TIME AS THE HOSPITAL BOARD. 
A NEW POARD IS FORr-1 
ING AND SHOUID BE SEATED BY THE FIRST OF THE YEAR. 
ON THIS YEAR'S BALI01' THE ISSUE OF WHETHER OR Nor THE CITY CMNED HOSPITAL 
SHOUL':) BE SOID, LEASE, OR STAY AS IT IS. 
I NEED TO KlilOtv AS 
CANDIDATE FOR CITY COUNCIL MEMBER OF EL CENTRO, CALIFORNIA 
IF I HAVE A POSSIBLE PROBLEM OF CONFLICT OR INCa1PATIBILITY. 
I HAVE NOT BEEN 
ABLE TO RESOLVE THIS THROUGH OUR CITY ATTORNEY. 
I NEED TO KlilOtv THE ANSWER. 
IF YOU DO ~ 
IT IS A CONFLICT OR INCa1PATIBLE TO DO OOTH WHAT ARE MY 
OPI'IONS. 
SINCERELY; 
~~d~ 
PAMELA. A. GASPAR 
2048 w:rr..r.av DRIVE 
EL CENTRO, CALIFORNIA 
92243 
PHONE: 619-353-2728 (HOME) 
619-339-7271 (WORK)

sections.question: I WOULD LIKE TO KNOW IF THERE IS ANY FORM OF CONFLICT TO BE AN EMPLOYEE OF A CITY OWNED HOSPITAL AND AT THE SAME TIME A MEMBER OF CITY COUNCIL. THE CITY COUNCIL SITS AT THIS TIME AS THE HOSPITAL BOARD. A NEW BOARD IS FORMING AND SHOULD BE SEATED BY THE FIRST OF THE YEAR. ON THIS YEAR'S BALLOT THE ISSUE OF WHETHER OR NOT THE CITY OWNED HOSPITAL SHOULD BE SOLD, LEASE, OR STAY AS IT IS. I NEED TO KNOW AS CANDIDATE FOR CITY COUNCIL MEMBER OF EL CENTRO, CALIFORNIA IF I HAVE A POSSIBLE PROBLEM OF CONFLICT OR INCOMPATIBILITY.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, the income you receive as an employee of the city-owned hospital is not considered income for purposes of the Political Reform Act. Accordingly, should you be successful in your bid for a council seat, you would not be required to disqualify yourself from decisions which may affect the hospital.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a person simultaneously serve as an employee of a city-owned hospital and as a member of the city council, particularly when the council currently sits as the hospital board and there is a ballot measure regarding the hospital's status?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded it lacks authority to advise on public office incompatibility issues, which fall under the Attorney General's jurisdiction. However, under the Political Reform Act, salary from a city-owned hospital is not considered financial interest income, so Ms. Gaspar would not be required to disqualify herself from hospital-related decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (dated September 9, 1985) and FPPC response letter (dated October 3, 1985). Response indicates FPPC defers to Attorney General's office for public office incompatibility questions but provides Political Reform Act guidance. Minor OCR issues in original document ('toreseeable' likely 'foreseeable', 'aqqreqatinq' likely 'aggregating').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I WOULD LIKE TO KNOW IF THERE IS ANY FORM OF CONFLICT TO BE AN EMPLOYEE OF A CITY OWNED HOSPITAL AND AT THE SAME TIME A MEMBER OF CITY COUNCIL. THE CITY COUNCIL SITS AT THIS TIME AS THE HOSPITAL BOARD. A NEW BOARD IS FORMING AND SHOULD BE SEATED BY THE FIRST OF THE YEAR. ON THIS YEAR'S BALLOT THE ISSUE OF WHETHER OR NOT THE CITY OWNED HOSPITAL SHOULD BE SOLD, LEASE, OR STAY AS IT IS. I NEED TO KNOW AS CANDIDATE FOR CITY COUNCIL MEMBER OF EL CENTRO, CALIFORNIA IF I HAVE A POSSIBLE PROBLEM OF CONFLICT OR INCOMPATIBILITY.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, the income you receive as an employee of the city-owned hospital is not considered income for purposes of the Political Reform Act. Accordingly, should you be successful in your bid for a council seat, you would not be required to disqualify yourself from decisions which may affect the hospital.

embedding.summary: Ms. Gaspar requested advice on whether serving as both a city-owned hospital employee and city council candidate presents conflicts of interest. The FPPC determined it lacks jurisdiction over public office incompatibility but clarified that government employment income is exempt from conflict of interest rules under the Political Reform Act.
